name: Appayya Dikshita
dates_notes: Court records of Cinna Bomma Nāyaka of Vellore; dates well-established.

dates_evidence.description: Court records of Cinna Bomma Nāyaka of Vellore attest patronage and dating.
Manuscript colophons across his many treatises confirm his floruit.

lineage_polemical.note: Appayya's Śivārka-Maṇi-Dīpikā engages the wider Śaiva tradition (Śaiva-Siddhānta and the Vīraśaiva movement) from a Smārta-Advaita-Śaiva position; the engagement with Vīraśaiva is partial but significant — Appayya treats the iṣṭa-liṅga theology as a popular-Śaiva variant whose metaphysical commitments deserve doctrinal scrutiny.

The seventeenth-century Vedāntic Navya-Nyāya commentaries take the developed Jaina-Navya-Nyāya position Yaśovijaya systematises as their target; the engagement is indirect (Appayya does not cite Yaśovijaya by name) but the school-level engagement runs through this Jaina-Navya-Nyāya stratum.

core_thesis: Appayya Dīkṣita is the late-sixteenth-century *Sarva-Tantra-Svatantra* whose corpus moves across schools. In the *Parimala* sub-commentary he writes as a *Bhāmatī*-line Advaitin; in the *Śivārka-Maṇi-Dīpikā* as a Śiva-Advaitin within Śrīkaṇṭha's framework; in the *Siddhānta-Leśa-Saṅgraha* as the canonical doxographer of intra-Advaita disagreement, cataloguing twenty-eight Advaitic disputes without forced reconciliation (*Siddhānta-Leśa-Saṅgraha*, prathama-paricheda through caturtha-paricheda). His distinctive contribution is methodological. Intra-Advaita differences — *Bhāmatī* vs. Vivaraṇa on the locus of *avidyā* (*Siddhānta-Leśa-Saṅgraha* prathama-paricheda, *avidyā-āśraya-vicāra*), *avaccheda-* vs. *pratibimba-vāda* (*Siddhānta-Leśa-Saṅgraha* prathama-paricheda, *jīva-svarūpa-vicāra*), the alternative analyses of the Mahāvākya's mode of operation (*Siddhānta-Leśa-Saṅgraha* dvitīya-paricheda, *vākyārtha-vicāra*) — are treated not as defects to be ironed out but as legitimate *prakriyā*-level alternatives within a single *siddhānta*. His prolific output, traditionally numbered at 104 works across six *darśanas* and reaching into Śaiva-Vedānta, Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, Alaṅkāra-śāstra, and devotional poetics, earns him the epithet *Sarva-Tantra-Svatantra*. He is the single most important late-medieval source for the structured comparative reading of competing Advaita sub-positions.

engaged_works.title: Siddhanta-Lesha-Samgraha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; catalogues internal Advaita disagreements.
engaged_works.summary: The canonical doxography of intra-Advaita disagreement: a structured catalogue of twenty-eight loci (in the standard reckoning) on which Advaita authors have differed, organised across four *parichedas*, with each locus presented as a *prakriyā*-level option set rather than a defect to be cured. Appayya works through the major disputed questions: the locus of *avidyā* (Bhāmatī's *jīva-āśraya* vs. Vivaraṇa's Brahman-*āśraya* — *Siddhānta-Leśa-Saṅgraha* prathama-paricheda, *avidyā-āśraya-vicāra*); the *avaccheda-*, *pratibimba-*, *ābhāsa-*, and *eka-jīva-vāda* alternatives for the *jīva* (prathama-paricheda, *jīva-svarūpa-vicāra*); the modes of operation of the Mahāvākya (dvitīya-paricheda, *vākyārtha-vicāra*); the *jīvanmukti* analyses (caturtha-paricheda, *jīvanmukti-vicāra*); the role of *karma* in producing the rise of *jñāna* (tṛtīya-paricheda, *jñāna-utpatti-vicāra*). He cites primary positions from Maṇḍana, Sureśvara, Vāchaspati, Sarvajñātman, Prakāśātman, Vimuktātman, Citsukha, and Vidyāraṇya. The work is the indispensable reference for any comparative analysis of Advaita sub-positions and the single most important pre-modern source for the structured study of intra-school disagreement. Acyutakṛṣṇānanda Tīrtha's *Kṛṣṇālaṅkāra* is the principal sub-commentary.

engaged_works.title: Parimala on Vachaspati's Bhamati
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary on Vāchaspati's Bhāmatī (via Amalānanda's Kalpataru).
engaged_works.summary: Sub-sub-commentary in the *Bhāmatī*-line transmission. It glosses Amalānanda's *Vedānta-Kalpataru*, which itself glosses Vāchaspati's *Bhāmatī*, which glosses Śaṅkara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*. Through this *tīkā-vyākhyā-vivṛti* layering Appayya commits to the *Bhāmatī* sub-school's positions — *jīva* as locus of *avidyā* (*Parimala* on *Bhāmatī* ad *Adhyāsa-Bhāṣya*), qualified *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* (*Parimala* on *Bhāmatī* ad BS 3.4), the *Bhāmatī* analysis of *adhyāsa* (*Parimala* on *Bhāmatī* ad *Adhyāsa-Bhāṣya*) — and refines them in light of the developed Vivaraṇa apparatus and the early Navya-Nyāya. The *Parimala* is the standard authoritative *Bhāmatī*-line treatment of the canonical *Brahma-Sūtra* loci in the late sixteenth century, and the principal reference through which subsequent *Bhāmatī* pedagogy is conducted. It shows Appayya at his most doctrinally committed, even as he writes elsewhere as Śiva-Advaitin and as neutral catalogue-keeper.

engaged_works.title: Sivarka-Mani-Dipika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Śiva-Advaita commentary on Śrīkaṇṭha's Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya.
engaged_works.summary: Major commentary on Śrīkaṇṭha's *Brahma-Mīmāṃsā-Bhāṣya*, the *Brahma-Sūtra* read through a Śaiva-Vedānta lens that identifies Brahman as Śiva. Appayya works the Śrīkaṇṭha system into a Śiva-Advaita with two levels. At the *paramārthika* level, Brahman / Śiva is the sole reality and the apparent personal Śiva of devotion is *vyāvahārika* (*Śivārka-Maṇi-Dīpikā* on Śrīkaṇṭha ad BS 1.1.2). At the *vyāvahārika* level, *Sa-Śiva* is the appropriate object of worship and the tradition's iconography and ritual practice retain their full validity (*Śivārka-Maṇi-Dīpikā* on Śrīkaṇṭha ad BS 4.4). The work matters as the canonical late-medieval Śiva-Advaita reading of the *Brahma-Sūtras*. It is also a window onto Appayya's larger trans-school *siddhānta*-discipline: the same author who in the *Parimala* defends *Bhāmatī*-Advaita and in the *Siddhānta-Leśa-Saṅgraha* catalogues neutral options here demonstrates how a sectarian Śaiva framework can be made internally consistent with Advaitic non-duality.

engaged_works.title: Ananda-Lahari-Vyakhya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in Appayya's mature period of Śaiva-Vedānta synthesis.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed to Appayya within the Śrīvidyā commentarial tradition; modern academic ascription accepts it as part of his Śaiva-devotional corpus though without the same documentary support as the Parimala or Siddhānta-Leśa-Saṃgraha.

ascription_evidence.description: Received within the Śrīvidyā and Śiva-Advaita commentarial traditions.
Manuscript transmission alongside other Śaiva works of Appayya.

engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the *Ānanda-Laharī* — the first half (verses 1–41) of the *Saundarya-Laharī* traditionally ascribed to Śaṅkara, devoted to the metaphysical-theological exposition of the Goddess (*Tripurasundarī* / *Lalitā*) as identical with the supreme, with the *śrī-cakra* (especially *Saundarya-Laharī* vv. 8–11, 32) and *kuṇḍalinī* references (*Saundarya-Laharī* vv. 9, 10, 35–41) that anchor the Śrīvidyā tradition. Appayya's *Vyākhyā* reads each verse simultaneously as devotional address, as Śrīvidyā ritual specification, and as Śiva-Advaita doctrinal exposition. The trans-school *siddhānta*-discipline already exercised in the *Śivārka-Maṇi-Dīpikā* is extended here into the devotional-poetic register. For each contested verse the commentary supplies three readings: a *Bhāmatī*-Advaita reading where the *Ānanda-Laharī*'s identity-language can be construed monistically (illustrative on *Saundarya-Laharī* v. 1, *śivaḥ śaktyā yukto*); a Śiva-Advaita reading where Śiva-Śakti polarity is in play; and a Śrīvidyā ritual reading where the verse encodes a *cakra*-element or *mantra*-component. The work is a key witness to Appayya's mature integration of Advaitic non-duality, Śaiva-devotional theology, and Śrīvidyā ritual within a single coherent *siddhānta*-frame, and occupies an important place in the late-medieval Śrīvidyā commentarial corpus.
